DIP: [{'DIP': 'The DIP class refers to the distal interphalangeal joints of the hand and foot, characterized by their small size, rounded shape, and the presence of a small, rounded articular surface at the distal end of the proximal phalanx. These joints are typically less prominent in X-ray images compared to the more elongated and cylindrical phalanges, and they are positioned at the end of the fingers and toes, where the fingers and toes meet the hand and foot. The DIP class is distinguished from other joints by its small size, rounded shape, and the presence of a small, rounded articular surface at the distal end of the proximal phalanx. The DIP class is further distinguished from the joints in the wrist and ankle areas by its location at the end of the fingers and toes, as opposed to the joints in the wrist and ankle areas. The DIP class is characterized by its proximity to the distal phalanges and the lack of any significant joint structure in the wrist and ankle areas. The DIP class is typically found in the distal phalanges of the fingers and toes, and it is the smallest joint in the hand and foot, with a rounded articular surface at the distal end of the proximal phalanx. The DIP class is not elongated or cylindrical like the phalanges, and it is not located in the wrist or ankle areas. The DIP class is further distinguished from the object in the red bounding box by its location at the end of the fingers and toes, as opposed to the object in the red bounding box, which is located in the wrist area.'}]
MCP: [{'MCP': 'The MCP class refers to the knuckles of the thumb and the first finger, which are formed by the protruding bones of the hand, specifically the metacarpals and phalanges. These knuckles are located at the base of the fingers, where the fingers meet the palm, and form a distinct curved line along the palm. The knuckles are composed of dense, white bone structures, which are more visible in X-ray-like images. The skin is partially covering the knuckles, appearing smooth and slightly raised around them. The MCP class excludes the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints, which are located further down the fingers and do not form a distinct curved line along the palm. The MCP class also excludes the wrist joint, which is located at the base of the hand and is not part of the knuckles. The MCP class includes the knuckles of the thumb and the first finger, as shown in the images.'}]
PIP: [{'PIP': 'The PIP joints are located in the middle of each finger, between the proximal and middle phalanges. They are small, rounded structures formed by the articulation of the middle phalanx and the proximal phalanx. These joints are surrounded by soft tissue, including the skin, subcutaneous fat, and the flexor and extensor tendons. They are typically not located at the base of the finger or at the base of the thumb, and they are not located in the palm of the hand. The PIP joints are characterized by their location in the middle of the fingers, their small size, and their rounded shape.'}]
Radius: [{'Radius': 'The Radius is a long, curved bone located at the wrist, forming the outer side of the wrist, connected to the ulna by a joint and articulates with the carpal bones. It is the largest and most prominent bone in the wrist, positioned more distally compared to the ulna and is the bone on the thumb side of the hand.'}]
Ulna: [{'Ulna': 'The ulna is a long, slender bone in the forearm, located on the medial (inner) side. It is part of the forearm and is connected to the radius at the elbow joint. The ulna is involved in the movement of the forearm and is a key component of the wrist joint. It is distinguished from the radius by its position on the medial side of the forearm and its involvement in the wrist joint. The ulna has a square shape with a prominent olecranon process at the elbow end and a smaller styloid process at the wrist end.'}]
Wrist: [{'Wrist': 'The wrist is a complex joint located at the base of the hand, where the hand meets the forearm. It is composed of multiple bones, including the carpal bones, which form a ring around the joint. The wrist is connected to the radius and ulna bones of the forearm, providing support and stability. It is characterized by a cluster of small, closely packed carpal bones that distinguish it from the larger bones of the hand and forearm. The wrist is typically situated between the hand and the forearm, and it is not confused with the forearm itself due to its distinct location and the presence of the carpal bones. The wrist is easily identifiable by its unique structure and the surrounding carpal bones, which are smaller and more numerous than the bones of the hand and forearm.'}]
